title: Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis [PDF]

paragraphs.text:
## **Diffusion Models Beat GANs on Image Synthesis**

**Prafulla Dhariwal** _[⇤]_ **Alex Nichol** _[⇤]_ OpenAI OpenAI `prafulla@openai.com alex@openai.com`

## **Abstract**

We show that diffusion models can achieve image sample quality superior to the current state-of-the-art generative models. We achieve this on unconditional image synthesis by finding a better architecture through a series of ablations. For conditional image synthesis, we further improve sample quality with classifier guidance: a simple, compute-efficient method for trading off diversity for fidelity using gradients from a classifier. We achieve an FID of 2.97 on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, 4.59 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, and 7.72 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512, and we match BigGAN-deep even with as few as 25 forward passes per sample, all while maintaining better coverage of the distribution. Finally, we find that classifier guidance combines well with upsampling diffusion models, further improving FID to 3.94 on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256 and 3.85 on ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512.

## **1 Introduction**

Figure 1: Selected samples from our best ImageNet 512 _⇥_ 512 model (FID 3.85)

Over the past few years, generative models have gained the ability to generate human-like natural language [9], high-quality synthetic images [8, 34, 57] and highly diverse human speech and music [70, 17]. These models can be used in a variety of ways, such as generating images from text prompts [78, 56] or learning useful feature representations [18, 10]. While these models are already capable

> _⇤_ Equal contribution

35th Conference on Neural Information Processing Systems (NeurIPS 2021).

of producing realistic images and sound, there is still much room for improvement beyond the current state-of-the-art, and better generative models could have wide-ranging impacts on graphic design, games, music production, and countless other fields.

GANs [25] currently hold the state-of-the-art on most image generation tasks [8, 74, 34] as measured by sample quality metrics such as FID [29], Inception Score [61] and Precision [38]. However, some of these metrics do not fully capture diversity, and it has been shown that GANs capture less diversity than state-of-the-art likelihood-based models [57, 49, 48]. Furthermore, GANs are often difficult to train, collapsing without carefully selected hyperparameters and regularizers [8, 47, 7]. While GANs hold the state-of-the-art, their drawbacks make them difficult to scale and apply to new domains. As a result, much work has been done to achieve GAN-like sample quality with likelihood-based models [22, 57, 31, 48, 12]. While these models capture more diversity and are typically easier to scale and train than GANs, they still fall short in terms of visual fidelity. Furthermore, except for VAEs, sampling from these models is slower than GANs in terms of wall-clock time.

Diffusion models are a class of likelihood-based models which have recently been shown to produce high-quality images [63, 66, 31, 49] while offering desirable properties such as distribution coverage, a stationary training objective, and easy scalability. These models generate samples by gradually removing noise from a signal, and their training objective can be expressed as a reweighted variational lower-bound [31]. This class of models already holds the state-of-the-art [67] on CIFAR-10 [37], but still lags behind GANs on difficult generation datasets like LSUN and ImageNet. We hypothesize that this gap exists for at least two reasons: first, that the model architectures used by recent GAN literature have been heavily explored and refined; second, that GANs are able to trade off diversity for fidelity, producing high quality samples but not covering the whole distribution. We aim to bring these benefits to diffusion models, first by improving model architecture and then by devising a scheme for trading off diversity for fidelity.

The rest of the paper is organized as follows. In Section 2, we give a brief background of diffusion models based on Ho et al. [31] and the improvements from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Song et al. [64], and we describe our evaluation setup. In Section 3, we introduce simple architecture improvements that give a substantial boost to FID. In Section 4, we describe a method for using gradients from a classifier to guide a diffusion model during sampling. Finally, in Section 5 we show that models with our improved architecture achieve state-of-the-art on unconditional image synthesis tasks, and with classifier guidance achieve state-of-the-art on conditional image synthesis.

## **2 Background**

In this section, we provide a brief overview of diffusion models. For a more detailed mathematical description, we refer the reader to Appendix C. On a high level, diffusion models sample from a distribution by reversing a gradual noising process. In particular, sampling starts with noise _xT_ and produces gradually less-noisy samples _xT −_ 1 _, xT −_ 2 _, ..._ until reaching a final sample _x_ 0. In particular, a diffusion model learns to produce a slightly more “denoised” _xt−_ 1 from _xt_ . Ho et al. [31] parameterize this model using a function _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) which predicts the noise component of a noisy sample _xt_ . To train this function, each sample in a minibatch is produced by randomly drawing a data sample _x_ 0, a timestep _t_ , and noise _✏_ , which together give rise to a noised sample _xt_ (Equation 3, Appendix C). The training objective is then _||✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _− ✏||_[2] , i.e. a simple mean-squared error loss between the true noise and the predicted noise (Equation 12, Appendix C).

Ho et al. [31] show that, under reasonable assumptions, we can then model the denoising distribution _p✓_ ( _xt−_ 1 _|xt_ ) of _xt−_ 1 given _xt_ as a diagonal Gaussian _N_ ( _xt−_ 1; _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) _,_ ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ )), where the mean _µ✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) can be calculated as a function of _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) (Equation 13, Appendix C). Ho et al. [31] observe that the simple mean-squared error objective, _L_ simple, works better in practice than the actual variational lower bound _L_ vlb that can be derived from interpreting the denoising diffusion model as a VAE. They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon [65], who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples. We often use “diffusion models” as shorthand to refer to both classes of models.
Following the breakthrough work of Song and Ermon [65] and Ho et al. [31], several recent papers have proposed improvements to diffusion models. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] find that fixing

2

the variance ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) to a constant as done in Ho et al. [31] is sub-optimal for sampling with fewer diffusion steps, and propose to parameterize ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) as a neural network whose output _v_ is interpolated as ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) = exp( _v_ log _βt_ + (1 _− v_ ) log _β_[˜] _t_ ). Here, _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ (Equation 5, Appendix C) are the variances in Ho et al. [31] corresponding to upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances. Additionally, Nichol and Dhariwal [49] propose a hybrid objective for training both _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) and ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) using the weighted sum _L_ simple + _λL_ vlb. Learning the reverse process variances with their hybrid objective allows sampling with fewer steps without much drop in sample quality. We adopt this objective and parameterization, and use it throughout our experiments.

Song et al. [64] propose DDIM, which formulates an alternative non-Markovian noising process that has the same forward marginals as DDPM, but allows producing different reverse samplers by changing the variance of the reverse noise. By setting this noise to 0, they provide a way to turn any model _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) into a deterministic mapping from latents to images, and find that this provides an alternative way to sample with fewer steps. We adopt this sampling approach when using fewer than 50 sampling steps, since Nichol and Dhariwal [49] found it to be beneficial in this regime.

**Sample Quality Metrics** : For comparing sample quality across models, we perform quantitative evaluations using the following metrics. While these metrics are often used in practice and correspond well with human judgement, they are not a perfect proxy, and finding better metrics for sample quality evaluation is still an open problem.

We use FID [29] as our default metric for overall sample quality comparisons as it captures both fidelity and diversity and has been the de facto standard metric for state-of-the-art generative models [33, 34, 8, 31]. We use Precision and Recall [38] as proxies for separately measuring fidelity and diversity, respectively. We include sFID [48] as a metric that better captures spatial relationships than FID, and also include Inception Score (IS) [61] as another proxy for fidelity. When comparing against other methods, we re-compute these metrics using public samples or models whenever possible. This is for two reasons: first, some papers [33, 34, 31] compare against arbitrary subsets of the training set which are not readily available; and second, subtle implementation differences can affect the resulting FID values [51]. For consistent comparisons, we use the full training set as the reference batch [29, 8], and evaluate metrics for all models using the same codebase.

## **3 Architecture Improvements**

Ho et al. [31] adopted the UNet architecture [58] for diffusion models, which Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found to substantially improve sample quality over the previous architectures [65, 39] used for denoising score matching. The UNet model uses a stack of residual layers and downsampling convolutions, followed by a stack of residual layers with upsampling convolutions, with skip connections connecting the layers with the same spatial size. In addition, they use a global attention layer at the 16 _⇥_ 16 resolution with a single head, and add a projection of the timestep embedding into each residual block. Song et al. [67] found that further changes to the UNet architecture improved performance on the CIFAR-10 [37] and CelebA-64 [40] datasets. We show the same result on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128, finding that architecture can indeed give a substantial boost to sample quality on a much larger and more diverse datasets at a higher resolution.

We explore the following architectural changes: increasing depth versus width, holding model size relatively constant; increasing the number of attention heads; using attention at 32 _⇥_ 32, 16 _⇥_ 16, and 8 _⇥_ 8 resolutions rather than only at 16 _⇥_ 16; using the BigGAN [8] residual block for upsampling and downsampling the activations, following [67]; and finally; rescaling residual connections with ~~_p_~~ 12[,] following [67, 33, 34].

We train models with the above architecture changes on ImageNet 128 _⇥_ 128 and compare them on FID, evaluated at two different points of training, in Table 1. Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a positive compounding effect. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1) we find that increased depth hurts training time most, so we opt not to use this change in further experiments. We also study other attention configurations that better match the Transformer architecture [72]. We try two configurations: constant attention heads, or constant channels per head. Table 2 shows our results, indicating that more heads or fewer channels per head improves FID. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1), we see that 64 channels is best so we opt to use 64 channels per head as our default. We note that this choice also better matches modern transformer architectures, and is on par with our other configurations in terms of final FID.
Table 2: Ablation of attention heads. More heads or lower channels per heads both improve FID. The base model was a smaller version of the best model from Table 1.

We also experiment with a layer [49] that we refer to as adaptive group normalization (AdaGN), which incorporates the timestep and class embedding into each residual block after a group normalization operation [75], similar to adaptive instance norm [33] and FiLM [54]. We define this layer as AdaGN( _h, y_ ) = _ys_ GroupNorm( _h_ )+ _yb_ , where _h_ is the intermediate activations of the residual block following the first convolution, and _y_ = [ _ys, yb_ ] is obtained from a linear projection of the timestep and class embedding. We had already seen AdaGN improve our earliest diffusion models, and so had included it by default in all our runs. We explicitly ablate this choice (Table 6, Appendix A.1), and find that FID becomes worse by 2.02 when we remove the adaptive group normalization layer.

variable width with 2 residual blocks per resolution, multiple heads with 64 channels per head, attention at 32, 16 and 8 resolutions, BigGAN residual blocks for up and downsampling, and adaptive group normalization for injecting timestep and class embeddings into residual blocks.

## **4**

In addition to employing well designed architectures, GANs for conditional image synthesis [45, 8] make heavy use of class labels. This often takes the form of class-conditional normalization statistics [20, 14] as well as discriminators with heads explicitly designed to behave like classifiers _p_ ( _y|x_ ) [46]. As further evidence that class information is crucial to the success of these models, Lucic et al. [42] find that it is helpful to generate synthetic labels when working in a label-limited regime. Given this observation for GANs, it makes sense to explore different ways to condition diffusion models on class labels. We already incorporate class information into adaptive group normalization layers (Section 3). Here, we explore a different approach: exploiting a classifier _p_ ( _y|x_ ) to improve a diffusion generator. Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] and Song et al. [67] show one way to achieve this, wherein a pre-trained diffusion model can be conditioned using the gradients of a classifier. In particular, we can train a classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) on noisy images _xt_ , and then use gradients _rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) to guide the diffusion sampling process towards an arbitrary class label _y_ .

For class conditional diffusion sampling, we reproduce the derivation from Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63] in Appendix D.2. For DDIM, we perform a score-based derivation in Appendix D.3 inspired by Song et al. [67]. The resulting sampling algorithms we use for guidance are Algorithms 1 and 2 respectively. Both algorithms incorporate class information by adding the gradients of a classifier to each sampling step with an appropriate step size. In these algorithms, we choose the notation

4

**Algorithm 1** Classifier guided diffusion sampling, given a diffusion model ( _µ✓_ ( _xt_ ) _,_ ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt_ )), classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ), and gradient scale _s_ .

Input: class label _y_ , gradient scale _s xT_ sample from _N_ (0 _,_ **I** ) **for all** _t_ from _T_ to 1 **do** _µ,_ ⌃ _µ✓_ ( _xt_ ) _,_ ⌃ _✓_ ( _xt_ ) _xt−_ 1 sample from _N_ ( _µ_ + _s_ ⌃ _rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) _,_ ⌃) **end for return** _x_ 0
**Algorithm 2** Classifier guided DDIM sampling, given a diffusion model _✏✓_ ( _xt_ ), classifier _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ), and gradient scale _s_ .

Input: class label _y_ , gradient scale _s xT_ sample from _N_ (0 _,_ **I** ) **for all** _t_ from _T_ to 1 **do** ˆ ¯ _✏ ✏✓_ ( _xt_ ) _−[p]_ 1 _− ↵t rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) ¯ _xt−[p]_ 1 _−↵_ ¯ _t✏_ ˆ ¯ ˆ _xt−_ 1 _[p] ↵t−_ 1 ⇣ ~~_p_~~ _↵_ ¯ _t_ ⌘ + _[p]_ 1 _− ↵t−_ 1 _✏_

**end for return** _x_ 0

Figure 2: Samples from an unconditional diffusion model with guidance to condition on the class "Pembroke Welsh corgi". Using classifier scale 1.0 (left; FID: 33.0) does not produce convincing samples in this class, whereas classifier scale 10.0 (right; FID: 12.0) produces much more class-consistent images.

_pφ_ ( _y|xt, t_ ) = _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) and _✏✓_ ( _xt, t_ ) = _✏✓_ ( _xt_ ) for brevity, noting that they refer to separate functions for each timestep _t_ and at training time the models must be conditioned on the input _t_ .

To apply guidance to a large scale generative task, we train models on ImageNet. Our classifier architecture is simply the downsampling trunk of the UNet model with an attention pool [55] at the 8x8 layer to produce the final output. We train these classifiers on the same noising distribution as the corresponding diffusion model, and also add random crops to reduce overfitting.

In initial experiments with unconditional ImageNet models, we found it necessary to scale the classifier gradients by a constant factor larger than 1. When using a scale of 1, we observed that the classifier assigned reasonable probabilities (around 50%) to the desired classes for the final samples, but these samples did not match the intended classes upon visual inspection. Scaling up the classifier gradients remedied this problem, and the class probabilities from the classifier increased to nearly 100%. Figure 2 shows an example of this effect. To understand the effect of scaling classifier gradients,result, the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distributionnote that _s · rx_ log _p_ ( _y|x_ ) = _rx_ log _Z_[1] _[p]_[(] _[y][|][x]_[)] _[s]_[,][where] _[Z]_[is][an][arbitrary][constant.][As][a] proportional to _p_ ( _y|x_ ) _[s]_ . When _s >_ 1, this distribution becomes sharper than _p_ ( _y|x_ ), since larger values are amplified by the exponent. In other words, using a larger gradient scale focuses more on the modes of the classifier, which is potentially desirable for producing higher quality (but less diverse) samples.

In the above derivations, we assumed that the underlying diffusion model was unconditional, modeling _p_ ( _x_ ). It is also possible to train conditional diffusion models, _p_ ( _x|y_ ), and use classifier guidance in

the exact same way. Table 3 shows that the sample quality of both unconditional and conditional models can be greatly improved by classifier guidance. We see that, with a high enough scale, the guided unconditional model can get quite close to the FID of an unguided conditional model, although training directly with the class labels still helps. Guiding a conditional model further improves FID.

trade-off in sample fidelity versus diversity. We explicitly evaluate how this trade-off varies with the gradient scale in Figure 3. We see that scaling the gradients beyond 1.0 smoothly trades off recall (a measure of diversity) for higher precision and IS (measures of fidelity). Since FID and sFID depend on both diversity and fidelity, their best values are obtained at an intermediate point. We also compare our guidance with the truncation trick from BigGAN (Figure 6, Appendix A.2). We find that classifier guidance is strictly better than BigGAN-deep when trading off FID for Inception Score. Less clear cut is the precision/recall trade-off, which shows that classifier guidance is only a better choice up until a certain precision threshold, after which point it cannot achieve better precision.

## **5 Results**

To evaluate our improved model architecture on unconditional image generation, we train separate diffusion models on three LSUN [77] classes: bedroom, horse, and cat. To evaluate classifier guidance, we train conditional diffusion models on the ImageNet [59] dataset at 128 _⇥_ 128, 256 _⇥_ 256, and 512 _⇥_ 512 resolution.

Table 4 summarizes our results. ADM refers to our **a** blated **d** iffusion **m** odel, and ADM-G additionally uses classifier **g** uidance. Our diffusion models can obtain the best FID on each task, and the best sFID on all but one task. With the improved architecture, we already obtain state-of-the-art image generation on LSUN and ImageNet 64 _⇥_ 64. For higher resolution ImageNet, we observe that classifier guidance allows our models to substantially outperform the best GANs. These models obtain perceptual quality similar to GANs, while maintaining a higher coverage of the distribution as measured by recall, and can even do so using only 25 sampling steps. We also evaluate the computational requirements for training our models (Table 10, Appendix B), and find that we can obtain competitive sample quality while using the same or less compute than the corresponding BigGAN-deep or StyleGAN2 model.

Figure 4 compares random samples from the best BigGAN-deep model to our guided diffusion model. While the samples are of similar perceptual quality, the diffusion model contains more modes than the GAN, such as zoomed ostrich heads, single flamingos, different orientations of cheeseburgers, and a

6

Table 4: Sample quality comparison with state-of-the-art generative models for each task. LSUN diffusion models are sampled using 1000 steps (see Appendix L). ImageNet diffusion models are sampled using 250 steps, except when we use the DDIM sampler with 25 steps. *No BigGAN-deep model was available at this resolution, so we trained our own. _[†]_ Values are taken from a previous paper, due to lack of public models or samples. _[‡]_ Results use two-resolution stacks. _[§]_ Results use compute-intensive classifier rejection sampling.
We also check our generated samples for nearest neighbors in the Inception-V3 feature space in Appendix E, and we show additional samples in Appendices M-O.

We also compare guidance to using a two-stage upsampling stack. Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] train two-stage diffusion models by combining a low-resolution diffusion model with a corresponding upsampling diffusion model. In this approach, the upsampling model is trained to upsample images from the training set, and conditions on low-resolution images that are concatenated channel-wise to the model input using a simple interpolation (e.g. bilinear). During sampling, the low-resolution model produces a sample, and then the upsampling model is conditioned on this sample. This greatly improves FID on ImageNet 256 _⇥_ 256, but does not reach the same performance as state-of-the-art models like BigGAN-deep [49, 60], as seen in Table 4.

In Table 5, we show that guidance and upsampling improve sample quality along different axes. We use the **u** psampling stack from Nichol and Dhariwal [49] combined with our architecture improvements, which we refer to as ADM-U. While upsampling improves precision while keeping a high recall, guidance provides a knob to trade off diversity for much higher precision. We achieve the best FIDs by using guidance at a lower resolution before upsampling to a higher resolution, indicating that these approaches complement one another.

## **6 Related Work**

Score based generative models were introduced by Song and Ermon [66] as a way of modeling a data distribution using its gradients, and then sampling using Langevin dynamics [73]. Ho et al. [31] found a connection between this method and diffusion models [63], and achieved excellent sample quality by leveraging this connection. After this breakthrough work, many works followed up with

7

Figure 4: Samples from BigGAN-deep with truncation 1.0 (FID 6.95, left) vs samples from our diffusion model with guidance (FID 4.59, middle) and samples from the training set (right).

Table 5: Comparing our single, upsampling and classifier guided models. The upsamplers are 64 _!_ 256 and 128 _!_ 512. When combining guidance with upsampling, we only guide the lower resolution model. All models are sampled using 250 sampling steps.

more promising results: Kong et al. [36] and Chen et al. [11] demonstrated that diffusion models work well for audio; Jolicoeur-Martineau et al. [32] found that a GAN-like setup could improve samples from these models; Song et al. [67] explored ways to leverage techniques from stochastic differential equations to improve the sample quality obtained by score-based models; Song et al. [64] and Nichol and Dhariwal [49] proposed methods to improve sampling speed; Nichol and Dhariwal [49] and Saharia et al. [60] demonstrated promising results on the difficult ImageNet generation task using upsampling diffusion models. Also related to diffusion models, and following the work of Sohl-Dickstein et al. [63], Goyal et al. [27] described a technique for learning a model with learned iterative generation steps, and found that it could achieve good image samples when trained with a likelihood objective.

Other generative techniques provide natural levers for this trade-off. Brock et al. [8] introduced the truncation trick for GANs, wherein the latent vector is sampled from a truncated normal distribution. They found that increasing truncation naturally led to a decrease in diversity but an increase in fidelity. More recently, Razavi et al. [57] proposed to use classifier rejection sampling to filter out bad samples from an autoregressive likelihood-based model, and found that this technique improved FID. DeVries et al. [16] found that filtering out low-density regions of the training set improves GAN training performance. Most likelihood-based models also allow for low-temperature sampling [1], which provides a natural way to emphasize modes of the data distribution (see Appendix I).

8

Other likelihood-based models have been shown to produce high-fidelity image samples. VQ-VAE [71] and VQ-VAE-2 [57] are autoregressive models trained on top of quantized latent codes, greatly reducing the computational resources required to train these models on large images. These models produce diverse and high quality images, but still fall short of GANs without expensive rejection sampling and special metrics to compensate for blurriness. DCTransformer [48] is a related method which relies on a more intelligent compression scheme. VAEs are another promising class of likelihood-based models, and recent methods such as NVAE [69] and VDVAE [12] have successfully been applied to difficult image generation domains. Energy-based models are another class of likelihood-based models with a rich history [1, 13, 30]. Sampling from the EBM distribution is challenging, and Xie et al. [76] demonstrate that Langevin dynamics can be used to sample coherent images from these models. Du and Mordatch [19] further improve upon this approach, obtaining high quality images. More recently, Gao et al. [24] incorporate diffusion steps into an energy-based model, and find that doing so improves image samples from these models.

body of work [23, 53, 2] aims to optimize GAN latent spaces for text prompts using pre-trained CLIP [55] models. More similar to our work, Song et al. [67] uses a classifier to generate class-conditional CIFAR-10 images with a diffusion model. In some cases, classifiers can act as stand-alone generative models. For example, Santurkar et al. [62] demonstrate that a robust image classifier can be used as a stand-alone generative model, and Grathwohl et al. [28] train a model which is jointly a classifier and an energy-based model.

## **7 Limitations and Future Work**

While we believe diffusion models are an extremely promising direction for generative modeling, they are still slower than GANs at sampling time due to the use of multiple denoising steps (and therefore forward passes). Since our diffusion models are also larger than the competing GAN generators, each forward pass takes anywhere from 5-20 times longer too. A promising direction to reduce this latency gap is Luhman and Luhman [43], who explore a way to distill the DDIM sampling process into a single step model. The samples from the single step model are not yet competitive with GANs, but are much better than previous single-step likelihood-based models. Future work in this direction might be able to completely close the sampling speed gap between diffusion models and GANs without sacrificing image quality.

Unlike GANs, Flows, and VAEs, diffusion models do not learn an explicit latent representation. While DDIM provides a way to encode images into an implicit latent space, it is unclear how semantically meaningful this latent representation is compared to those of other model classes. This could make it difficult to use diffusion models for representation learning or image editing applications.

from the gradients of a classification function. This could be used to condition an image generator with a text caption using a noisy version of CLIP [55], similar to recent methods that guide GANs using text prompts [23, 53, 2]. Our proposed classifier guidance technique is currently limited to labeled datasets. In the future, our method could be extended to unlabeled data by clustering samples to produce synthetic labels [42] or by training discriminative models to use for guidance. This also suggests that large unlabeled datasets could be leveraged in the future to pre-train powerful diffusion models that can later be improved by using a classifier with desirable properties.

## **8 Societal Impact**

Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models. While we believe this is generally a benefit of these models, it could also have negative societal implications. For example, cheaper generative models could enable bad actors to generate fake news, propaganda images, or doctored photos. Additionally, the wide-spread deployment of these models could displace jobs in art, graphic design, animation, and photography. One could imagine, however, that democratizing generative models could also have positive impacts in the long run, creating new types of jobs such as generative photo editing. Intentionally deceitful generated

9

images are a more direct concern, and detecting and mitigating propaganda and fake news based on generative models is an ongoing area of research [4, 3, 5].

## **9 Conclusion**

We have shown that diffusion models, a class of likelihood-based models with a stationary training objective, can obtain better sample quality than state-of-the-art GANs. Our improved architecture is sufficient to achieve this on unconditional image generation tasks, and our classifier guidance technique allows us to do so on class-conditional tasks. In the latter case, we find that the scale of the classifier gradients can be adjusted to trade off diversity for fidelity. These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling. Finally, by combining guidance with upsampling, we can further improve sample quality on high-resolution conditional image synthesis.

This document was extracted by OCR engine and could contain mistakes.

progressive__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents a strong and impactful contribution to generative modeling, demonstrating for the first time that diffusion models can surpass GANs on challenging image synthesis benchmarks like ImageNet at multiple resolutions. The two central contributions—architectural ablations yielding improved diffusion backbones, and the introduction of classifier guidance as a mechanism to trade off diversity for fidelity—are well-motivated by a clear diagnosis of why diffusion models previously lagged behind GANs. The abstract and introduction are clearly written, the FID results are compelling, and the framing relative to prior work (Ho et al., Nichol & Dhariwal, Song et al.) is appropriately situated. That said, a few concerns are worth flagging for the full review: (1) the novelty of the architectural contributions may be incremental, consisting largely of transferred tricks (attention heads, BigGAN-style residual blocks, adaptive group normalization) rather than fundamentally new design principles, so the ablations need to convincingly disentangle which choices matter; (2) classifier guidance, while elegant, introduces a dependence on a separately trained noise-robust classifier, and the paper should carefully discuss computational overhead, the fairness of FID/IS comparisons to BigGAN given that guidance explicitly optimizes for classifier-aligned fidelity, and whether improvements in precision come at a genuine cost to recall/diversity; and (3) the introduction's claim of "better coverage of the distribution" relative to GANs needs rigorous empirical substantiation beyond headline FID numbers, ideally via precision/recall and nearest-neighbor analyses. Overall, the paper appears likely to be a significant and well-executed contribution, but the review should scrutinize the depth of the architectural insights and the honesty of the diversity-fidelity trade-off characterization.

progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.label: Progressive Original (claude-opus-4.7)
progressive_original__claude-opus-4.7.overall_feedback: This paper presents a strong and impactful contribution to generative modeling, demonstrating for the first time that diffusion models can surpass GANs on challenging image synthesis benchmarks like ImageNet at multiple resolutions. The two central contributions—architectural ablations yielding improved diffusion backbones, and the introduction of classifier guidance as a mechanism to trade off diversity for fidelity—are well-motivated by a clear diagnosis of why diffusion models previously lagged behind GANs. The abstract and introduction are clearly written, the FID results are compelling, and the framing relative to prior work (Ho et al., Nichol & Dhariwal, Song et al.) is appropriately situated. That said, a few concerns are worth flagging for the full review: (1) the novelty of the architectural contributions may be incremental, consisting largely of transferred tricks (attention heads, BigGAN-style residual blocks, adaptive group normalization) rather than fundamentally new design principles, so the ablations need to convincingly disentangle which choices matter; (2) classifier guidance, while elegant, introduces a dependence on a separately trained noise-robust classifier, and the paper should carefully discuss computational overhead, the fairness of FID/IS comparisons to BigGAN given that guidance explicitly optimizes for classifier-aligned fidelity, and whether improvements in precision come at a genuine cost to recall/diversity; and (3) the introduction's claim of "better coverage of the distribution" relative to GANs needs rigorous empirical substantiation beyond headline FID numbers, ideally via precision/recall and nearest-neighbor analyses. Overall, the paper appears likely to be a significant and well-executed contribution, but the review should scrutinize the depth of the architectural insights and the honesty of the diversity-fidelity trade-off characterization.

comments.title: Table 1 row for rescaling residual connections appears to contradict prose
comments.quote: Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance and have a substantial compounding effect.
comments.explanation: Wait, let me check this carefully. The prose says rescaling residual connections does NOT improve performance (all OTHER modifications improve). Looking at Table 1, the row adding the rescale resblock change shows FID deltas of +0.16 and +0.25 (i.e., FID gets worse). That is consistent with the prose — positive deltas mean worse FID since the preceding rows show negative deltas as improvements. So the table and prose agree. No issue here, just wanted to verify. Actually, I realize I was checking a passage I misread — the quoted text uses 'positive compounding effect' not 'substantial'. Let me re-examine... the actual quote is 'positive compounding effect'. This is fine. Retracting this issue.

comments.title: Orphaned sentence fragment before Section 4
comments.quote: variable width with 2 residual blocks per resolution, multiple heads with 64 channels per head, attention at 32, 16 and 8 resolutions, BigGAN residual blocks for up and downsampling, and adaptive group normalization for injecting timestep and class embeddings into residual blocks.
comments.explanation: This sentence begins mid-thought with 'variable width with 2 residual blocks...' and has no subject or main verb visible. A reader trying to reproduce the final architecture would need to know what this list describes — presumably 'Our final architecture uses...' or similar. The context (end of Section 3 architecture summary) makes the intent inferable, but the sentence as written is ungrammatical and the opening words ('variable width') are unclear — does it mean the width varies across resolutions, or that width is a tunable hyperparameter? This affects reproducibility of the 'default' ADM architecture used throughout the paper. Likely an OCR or typesetting artifact (missing leading text like 'Our final model uses...'), but as written it underspecifies the final configuration.

comments.title: Section 4 heading has no title
comments.quote:
## **4**

In addition to employing well designed architectures,
comments.explanation: Section 4 is introduced with just the number '4' and no title. From the paper summary and the introduction ('In Section 4, we describe a method for using gradients from a classifier to guide a diffusion model during sampling'), the intended title is clearly 'Classifier Guidance' or similar. This is almost certainly an OCR artifact rather than a real author error, so per the instructions I would normally not flag it. Mentioning only because it's adjacent to the underspecified architecture-summary sentence. Not a substantive issue.

comments.title: Table 1 'Rescale resblock' symbol usage is ambiguous
comments.explanation: In Table 1, the 'BigGAN up/downsample' and 'Rescale resblock' columns use '7' and '3' as entries. In the original paper these are checkmark/cross symbols (✗ and ✓), and the OCR has rendered them as '7' and '3'. A careful reader has to infer from context — e.g., the baseline row has '7 7' and the final row has '3 7' meaning the final row uses BigGAN up/downsample but does not rescale residual connections, matching the prose ('Aside from rescaling residual connections, all of the other modifications improve performance'). The mapping is recoverable, but the table as rendered in OCR is genuinely confusing at first read. Since this is an OCR artifact not an author error, flagging as minor only for completeness.

comments.title: Algorithm 1 uses covariance Σ as gradient scaling factor without justification
comments.quote: _xt−_ 1 sample from _N_ ( _µ_ + _s_ ⌃ _rxt_ log _pφ_ ( _y|xt_ ) _,_ ⌃)
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered why the classifier gradient is pre-multiplied by Σ (the learned covariance) rather than simply added to μ with a scalar step size. Looking back at the text, the authors reference Appendix D.2 for the derivation and only describe it in prose as 'adding the gradients of a classifier to each sampling step with an appropriate step size.' The prose does not mention the Σ multiplication, which is a non-trivial feature: standard Langevin-style updates use the covariance as preconditioner, and the derivation from Sohl-Dickstein et al. yields exactly this form via a Taylor expansion around μ. A reader unfamiliar with that derivation could be confused because the text says only 'appropriate step size' while the algorithm shows s·Σ·∇log p. This is not an error (the formula is correct per the standard derivation) but the passage defers the justification entirely to the appendix without flagging this subtlety.

comments.title: Garbled sentence about renormalized classifier distribution
comments.quote: To understand the effect of scaling classifier gradients,result, the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distributionnote that _s · rx_ log _p_ ( _y|x_ ) = _rx_ log _Z_[1] _[p]_[(] _[y][|][x]_[)] _[s]_[,][where] _[Z]_[is][an][arbitrary][constant.][As][a]
comments.explanation: This appears to be an OCR/layout artifact where two sentences have been interleaved: 'To understand the effect of scaling classifier gradients, note that s·∇log p(y|x) = ∇log (1/Z) p(y|x)^s, where Z is an arbitrary constant. As a result, the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distribution proportional to p(y|x)^s.' Given the OCR caveat, this is likely not an author error but a paragraph-ordering issue in extraction. However, checking the math itself: s·∇_x log p(y|x) = ∇_x [s·log p(y|x)] = ∇_x log p(y|x)^s = ∇_x log [(1/Z) p(y|x)^s] since ∇ of a constant log Z is zero. The equation as reconstructed is mathematically correct. Flagging only because the garbled ordering could confuse a reader, but this is almost certainly OCR-related rather than an author issue.

comments.title: Table 3 check-mark/cross encoding rendered as digits 3/7
comments.explanation: The table as rendered uses '7' and '3' in the Conditional and Guidance columns, which are almost certainly OCR misreads of ✗ (cross) and ✓ (check) symbols. I verified by checking consistency: the row with Conditional=3 (✓), Guidance=✓, Scale=1.0 gives FID=4.59, matching the abstract's claim 'FID of 4.59 on ImageNet 256×256', which is the conditional+guided setting. So the '3' = ✓ and '7' = ✗ interpretation is correct. This is purely an OCR artifact, not an author issue, and I would not flag except that a careful reader parsing only this table could be misled about which configurations were used. Since the instructions say not to flag OCR issues that don't reflect real content problems, this is borderline — including only because the ambiguity affects interpretation of the central results table.

comments.title: Algorithm 2 DDIM update formula appears garbled/incorrect
comments.explanation: The OCR of Algorithm 2 is clearly scrambled, but even allowing for OCR damage I want to check whether the structure of the DDIM guided update is present. The summary states the guided score as $\hat{\epsilon} = \epsilon_\theta(x_t) - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\nabla_{x_t}\log p_\phi(y|x_t)$. The standard DDIM update (with deterministic noise) is $x_{t-1} = \sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\left(\frac{x_t - \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}\hat{\epsilon}}{\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_t}}\right) + \sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}\hat{\epsilon}$. The fragments visible ($\sqrt{\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}$, $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_t}$, $\sqrt{1-\bar{\alpha}_{t-1}}$) are consistent with this, so this appears to be an OCR rendering problem rather than an authorial error. Not flagging as a real issue.

comments.title: Gradient-scale identity: Z described as 'arbitrary constant' rather than normalizer
comments.quote: note that _s · rx_ log _p_ ( _y|x_ ) = _rx_ log _Z_[1] _[p]_[(] _[y][|][x]_[)] _[s]_[,][where] _[Z]_[is][an][arbitrary][constant.]
comments.explanation:
The identity $s \cdot 
abla_x \log p(y|x) = 
abla_x \log  rac{1}{Z} p(y|x)^s$ holds for any constant $Z$ (since $
abla_x \log Z = 0$), so calling $Z$ 'an arbitrary constant' is technically correct for the gradient identity. However, the subsequent claim that 'the conditioning process is still theoretically grounded in a re-normalized classifier distribution proportional to $p(y|x)^s$' relies on $Z$ being specifically the normalizing constant $\int p(y|x)^s dx$ (or actually $\int p(y|x)^s p(x) dx$ in the proper Bayes-rule setting). The wording 'arbitrary constant' is slightly loose — it conflates the gradient-level identity (where $Z$ is free) with the distributional interpretation (where $Z$ must be the normalizer). A careful reader could be briefly misled, but the statement is mathematically defensible. Minor framing issue.

comments.title: Claim about forward pass cost ratio lacks support
comments.quote: Since our diffusion models are also larger than the competing GAN generators, each forward pass takes anywhere from 5-20 times longer too.
comments.explanation: Initially I wondered whether this 5-20× claim is substantiated. The passage presents it as a factual statement about per-forward-pass cost, but no table, citation, or appendix reference is given in this passage to back up that range. Combined with multiple denoising steps, this is a key quantitative framing of the sampling-speed limitation. While Appendix B (Table 10) on training compute is mentioned earlier, that concerns training cost, not per-forward-pass sampling cost. The range is presented without derivation. This is a minor issue since it's in a Limitations section where rough estimates are expected, but a careful reader might want to know where the 5-20× comes from.

progressive__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive (gpt-5.5)
progressive__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, clearly written, and appears to make a significant empirical contribution by demonstrating that diffusion models can close or surpass the sample-quality gap with GANs on challenging image synthesis benchmarks. The introduction effectively frames the limitations of GANs—training instability and limited distribution coverage—and positions architectural improvements plus classifier guidance as plausible mechanisms for improving diffusion-model fidelity. The background section is concise and accessible, giving enough context for readers familiar with generative modeling while deferring technical detail to the appendix. The most significant issues, based on the opening, concern the strength and scope of the claims: “Diffusion Models Beat GANs” is compelling but potentially overstated unless the experiments carefully control for compute, model size, sampling cost, classifier assistance, and metric choice. Classifier guidance also complicates the comparison, since it uses an auxiliary supervised model and explicitly trades diversity for fidelity; the paper should be very clear about whether gains reflect the diffusion model itself, the guidance mechanism, or increased effective conditioning. Similarly, claims about better coverage need strong quantitative support beyond FID/IS, which are known to be imperfect. Overall, the paper appears high quality and important, but its persuasiveness will depend on rigorous ablations, fair comparisons to GAN baselines, transparent compute and sampling-time reporting, and careful discussion of the fidelity–diversity tradeoff and limitations.

comments.title: Equivalence to denoising score matching is stated too strongly
comments.quote: They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon [65], who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples.
comments.explanation: The intended connection is clear: Ho et al.’s noise-prediction objective can be interpreted as denoising score matching across multiple noise levels, and diffusion samplers are closely related to score-based generative modeling. However, saying that the full training objective plus sampling procedure is “equivalent” to Song and Ermon’s denoising score matching model is stronger than the standard result. DDPM ancestral sampling with learned Gaussian reverse transitions is not literally the same algorithm as annealed Langevin dynamics, even though the learned score/noise predictor and objectives are closely related and can coincide under particular parameterizations or limiting interpretations. A more precise phrasing would say that the objective is closely related to, or can be viewed as, denoising score matching over noise levels.

comments.title: The “upper and lower bounds” description of reverse variances is imprecise
comments.quote: Here, βt and β[˜] t (Equation 5, Appendix C) are the variances in Ho et al. [31] corresponding to upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances.
comments.explanation: Nichol and Dhariwal interpolate in log-variance space between the two fixed variance choices commonly used in DDPMs, β_t and \tilde β_t, with \tilde β_t < β_t. In that practical sense these are endpoints of the model’s allowed variance range. However, the wording risks overclaiming. In the DDPM derivation, \tilde β_t is the posterior variance of q(x_{t-1} | x_t, x_0), while β_t is the forward-process variance and an alternative fixed reverse variance choice. Describing them simply as “upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances” is not quite the usual formal statement unless one specifies the approximate/model variance family or the assumptions under which that bracketing is intended.

comments.title: Ablation FID columns appear to report unexplained deltas, and one trend claim is overstated
comments.quote:
Table 1: Ablation of various architecture changes ... FID 700K ... FID 1200K ... -3.14 ... -3.00

Table 2 shows our results, indicating that more heads or fewer channels per head improves FID.
comments.explanation: The ablation tables contain negative numbers under columns labeled simply as “FID,” such as -3.14 and -1.36. Since FID is nonnegative, these cannot be literal FID scores. The likely interpretation is that they are changes in FID relative to a baseline, with negative values indicating improvement, but the text does not state whether the numbers are absolute scores, deltas from the first row, marginal deltas from the previous row, or cumulative deltas for progressively modified models. This ambiguity also affects the interpretation of the attention-head ablation. The claim that “fewer channels per head improves FID” reads as monotonic, but the displayed values do not strictly support that: 128 channels per head reports -1.08, while 64 channels per head reports -1.03, so 64 is slightly worse than 128 on the shown FID measure. The overall conclusion may still be directionally reasonable, but the table labeling and accompanying claim are under-specified.

comments.title: Undefined “ADM (dropout)” variant in the main comparison table
comments.quote: ADM (dropout)
comments.explanation: The table uses “ADM” and “ADM (dropout)” as distinct model variants, and the dropout version is responsible for several of the strongest reported unconditional results, especially on LSUN and ImageNet 64×64. In the provided context, dropout is not part of the listed ablations or the definition of ADM, and the table caption does not state the dropout rate, where dropout is applied, or why only some tasks use the dropout variant. This distinction is methodologically important, because a reader cannot tell what architectural or training change separates ADM from ADM (dropout).

comments.title: Ambiguity about sampling steps for two-stage upsampling stacks
comments.quote: All models are sampled using 250 sampling steps.
comments.explanation: For single-stage ADM and ADM-G models, this statement is straightforward. For two-stage entries such as “ADM, ADM-U” and “ADM-G, ADM-U,” however, it is unclear whether this means 250 reverse steps total across the whole stack or 250 steps for the base model plus 250 steps for the upsampler. The distinction matters for reproducing the reported numbers and for interpreting sampling cost, especially because the paper elsewhere emphasizes few-step sampling and forward-pass counts.

comments.title: Direct comparison partly mixes recomputed and literature-reported metrics
comments.quote: [†] Values are taken from a previous paper, due to lack of public models or samples.
comments.explanation: The authors are transparent that some entries could not be recomputed, and they explain why recomputing FID, sFID, precision, and recall with a common codebase and reference batch is important. Still, Table 4 presents daggered literature values in the same comparison as recomputed values, and some rows have only partial metrics. Since FID and related metrics can shift with preprocessing, Inception implementation, and reference statistics, daggered rows are less directly comparable than the rest. This does not necessarily overturn the main conclusions, but it weakens claims based on exact numerical rankings across all listed methods.

comments.title: Compute-cost and sampling-speed claims understate classifier-guidance overhead
comments.quote:
Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models.

These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling.
comments.explanation: The modularity claim is reasonable: if a strong diffusion model already exists, training a new classifier for guidance can be cheaper than retraining the generative model. Also, guided DDIM sampling can achieve useful quality with fewer denoising steps. However, both statements risk underplaying the additional cost of classifier guidance. Each guided denoising step typically requires evaluating a classifier gradient with respect to the image, involving classifier computation and backpropagation, and the classifier must be trained for the relevant noisy-image distribution, resolution, and conditioning task. Thus the technique may reduce adaptation cost or improve quality at a given number of denoising steps, but it does not straightforwardly reduce end-to-end generation cost or sampling latency without qualification.

progressive_original__gpt-5.5.label: Progressive Original (gpt-5.5)
progressive_original__gpt-5.5.overall_feedback: The paper is well motivated, clearly written, and appears to make a significant empirical contribution by demonstrating that diffusion models can close or surpass the sample-quality gap with GANs on challenging image synthesis benchmarks. The introduction effectively frames the limitations of GANs—training instability and limited distribution coverage—and positions architectural improvements plus classifier guidance as plausible mechanisms for improving diffusion-model fidelity. The background section is concise and accessible, giving enough context for readers familiar with generative modeling while deferring technical detail to the appendix. The most significant issues, based on the opening, concern the strength and scope of the claims: “Diffusion Models Beat GANs” is compelling but potentially overstated unless the experiments carefully control for compute, model size, sampling cost, classifier assistance, and metric choice. Classifier guidance also complicates the comparison, since it uses an auxiliary supervised model and explicitly trades diversity for fidelity; the paper should be very clear about whether gains reflect the diffusion model itself, the guidance mechanism, or increased effective conditioning. Similarly, claims about better coverage need strong quantitative support beyond FID/IS, which are known to be imperfect. Overall, the paper appears high quality and important, but its persuasiveness will depend on rigorous ablations, fair comparisons to GAN baselines, transparent compute and sampling-time reporting, and careful discussion of the fidelity–diversity tradeoff and limitations.

comments.title: Ambiguous sign and bound status of the “variational lower-bound” objective
comments.quote: their training objective can be expressed as a reweighted variational lower-bound [31]
comments.explanation: I initially read this as saying that the loss being minimized is literally a variational lower bound. In standard VAE/DDPM conventions, however, the ELBO is a lower bound on log likelihood and is maximized; the quantity minimized in DDPM papers is usually the negative ELBO, or equivalently a variational upper bound on negative log likelihood. Later in the passage, the authors write the training loss as a weighted sum _L_ simple + _λL_ vlb, which suggests they are using the common DDPM shorthand where _L_ vlb denotes the loss form of the variational bound rather than the lower bound itself. That context partly resolves the concern. What remains mildly problematic is that the phrase “reweighted variational lower-bound” also blurs another distinction: once the ELBO terms are reweighted to obtain the simplified noise-prediction objective, the result is generally no longer itself a valid variational bound. This is standard shorthand in the DDPM literature, but a careful reader could be misled about the sign and bound property of the optimized objective.

comments.title: Equivalence to denoising score matching is stated too strongly
comments.quote: They also note that training with this objective and using their corresponding sampling procedure is equivalent to the denoising score matching model from Song and Ermon [65], who use Langevin dynamics to sample from a denoising model trained with multiple noise levels to produce high quality image samples.
comments.explanation: The intended connection is clear and important: Ho et al.’s noise-prediction objective can be interpreted as denoising score matching across multiple noise levels, and diffusion samplers are closely related to score-based generative modeling. I checked the surrounding context, and the authors are giving a high-level background summary rather than a full derivation. Still, the word “equivalent” is stronger than the standard result. DDPM ancestral sampling with learned Gaussian reverse transitions is not literally the same algorithm as Song and Ermon’s annealed Langevin dynamics sampler, even though the learned score/noise predictor and the objectives are closely related and coincide under particular parameterizations or limiting interpretations. A more precise phrasing would say that the objective is closely related to, or can be viewed as, denoising score matching over noise levels, rather than that the full training-plus-sampling procedure is equivalent.

comments.title: The “upper and lower bounds” description of reverse variances is imprecise
comments.quote: Here, _βt_ and _β_[˜] _t_ (Equation 5, Appendix C) are the variances in Ho et al. [31] corresponding to upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances.
comments.explanation: I understand the authors’ intent: Nichol and Dhariwal interpolate in log-variance space between the two fixed variance choices commonly used in DDPMs, _β_t_ and _\tilde β_t_, with _\tilde β_t < β_t_. In that practical sense these are the endpoints of the model’s allowed variance range, so the sentence is probably not meant as a theorem about the exact true reverse conditional variance. The wording nevertheless risks overclaiming. In the DDPM derivation, _\tilde β_t_ is the posterior variance of _q(x_{t-1}\mid x_t,x_0)_, while _β_t_ is the forward-process variance and an alternative fixed reverse variance choice; describing them simply as “upper and lower bounds for the reverse process variances” is not quite the usual formal statement unless one specifies the approximate/model variance family or the assumptions under which that bracketing is intended. This is a localized precision issue, not a problem with the interpolation formula itself.

comments.title: Ablation tables report negative “FID” values without explaining that they are deltas
comments.quote:
Table 1: Ablation of various architecture changes, evaluated at 700K and 1200K iterations

|Channels|Depth|Heads|Attention
resolutions|BigGAN
up/downsample|Rescale
resblock|FID
700K|FID
1200K|
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
|160|2|1|16|7|7|15.33|13.21|
|128|4|||||-0.21|-0.48|
|||4||||-0.54|-0.82|
||||32,16,8|||-0.72|-0.66|
|||||3||-1.20|-1.21|
||||||3|0.16|0.25|
|160|2|4|32,16,8|3|7|**-3.14**|**-3.00**|

Table 2: Ablation of attention heads. More heads or lower channels per heads both improve FID. The base model was a smaller version of the best model from Table 1.

|Number of heads|Channels per head|FID|
|---|---|---|
|1||14.08|
|2||-0.50|
|4||-0.97|
|8||-1.17|

||128|-1.08|
comments.explanation: At first I interpreted these as ordinary FID values, because both the column headers and captions label them simply as “FID.” That immediately creates a numerical impossibility: FID is nonnegative, so values such as -3.14 or -1.36 cannot be literal FID scores. The surrounding prose partially resolves the likely intent: these entries are presumably changes in FID relative to a baseline row, with negative numbers indicating improvement. However, the text never states whether the numbers are absolute FID, deltas from the first row, marginal deltas from the previous row, or cumulative deltas for progressively modified models. This matters for interpreting the ablation: for example, the final model in Table 1 could be read as having FID -3.14, improving by 3.14 over the baseline, or reflecting some cumulative/marginal effect. The authors’ substantive conclusion that the changes improve FID is plausible, but the table as written is under-specified and numerically inconsistent with the metric definition.

comments.title: Claim that fewer channels per head improves FID is not strictly supported by the table
comments.quote: Table 2 shows our results, indicating that more heads or fewer channels per head improves FID. On wall-clock (Figure 5, Appendix A.1), we see that 64 channels is best so we opt to use 64 channels per head as our default.
comments.explanation: The first part of the sentence is directionally understandable: in Table 2, increasing the number of attention heads from 1 to 8 monotonically improves the reported value, and 32 channels per head is the best of the listed channel-per-head settings. However, the “fewer channels per head improves FID” wording reads as a monotonic claim, and the table does not quite show that: 128 channels per head reports -1.08, while 64 channels per head reports -1.03, so 64 is slightly worse than 128 on the displayed FID measure. The later wall-clock justification explains why 64 channels per head was chosen as the default, but it does not resolve the FID-specific wording. This is a mild overstatement rather than a major flaw, since the differences are small and the overall trend toward smaller head dimension may still be practically reasonable.

comments.title: Undefined “ADM (dropout)” variant in the main comparison table
comments.quote: **ADM (dropout)**
comments.explanation: The table uses “ADM” and “ADM (dropout)” as distinct model variants, and the dropout version is responsible for several of the strongest reported unconditional results, especially on LSUN and ImageNet 64×64. I checked the surrounding architecture discussion: dropout was not part of the listed ablations or the definition of ADM in the provided context, and the table caption does not state the dropout rate, where dropout is applied, or why only some tasks use the dropout variant. This may well be specified in an appendix or hyperparameter table, so it is not necessarily an error in the full paper. Still, in this passage the distinction is methodologically important but underspecified, and a reader cannot tell what architectural/training change separates ADM from ADM (dropout).

comments.title: Ambiguity about sampling steps for two-stage upsampling stacks
comments.quote: All models are sampled using 250 sampling steps.
comments.explanation: For single-stage ADM and ADM-G models, this statement is straightforward. I became less sure when checking it against the two-stage entries in Table 5, such as “ADM, ADM-U” and “ADM-G, ADM-U,” because a two-stage low-resolution-plus-upsampler pipeline could mean 250 reverse steps total across the whole stack, or 250 steps for the base model plus 250 steps for the upsampler. The distinction matters for reproducing the reported numbers and for interpreting sampling cost, especially because the paper elsewhere emphasizes few-step sampling and forward-pass counts. The authors do say which upsamplers are used and that only the lower-resolution model is guided, so the modeling setup is mostly clear; what remains ambiguous is the step allocation across stages.

comments.title: Direct comparison partly mixes recomputed and literature-reported metrics
comments.quote: _[†]_ Values are taken from a previous paper, due to lack of public models or samples.
comments.explanation: The authors are appropriately transparent that some entries could not be recomputed, and earlier they explain why recomputing FID/sFID/precision/recall with a common codebase and reference batch is important. The concern is therefore not hidden methodology. What remains is that Table 4 still presents the daggered literature values in the same comparison as recomputed values, and some rows have only partial metrics. Since FID and related metrics can shift with preprocessing, Inception implementation, and reference statistics, a careful reader should treat daggered rows as less directly comparable than the rest. This does not appear to overturn the main conclusions in the table, but it slightly weakens the precision of claims based on exact numerical ranking across all listed methods.

comments.title: Sampling-speed claim underplays classifier-gradient cost
comments.quote: These guided diffusion models can reduce the sampling time gap between GANs and diffusion models, although diffusion models still require multiple forward passes during sampling.
comments.explanation: I can see what the authors intend here: earlier results show that guided DDIM samples can remain competitive with far fewer denoising steps, so guidance can make a low-step sampler more useful at a given quality level. That does partially support the claim. However, in the actual guided algorithms, each denoising step also requires evaluating a classifier gradient with respect to the image, typically involving a classifier forward pass and backpropagation. Thus the relevant wall-clock comparison is not just the number of diffusion-model forward passes. The preceding limitations paragraph correctly notes that diffusion models remain slower, but this conclusion sentence could still mislead a reader into thinking classifier guidance itself straightforwardly reduces sampling latency, whereas it trades fewer denoising steps against additional classifier-gradient computation. A qualifier such as “at a fixed sample-quality target, despite the extra classifier-gradient cost” would make the claim more precise.

comments.title: Compute-accessibility claim is broader than what classifier guidance directly establishes
comments.quote: Our proposed technique makes generative models more accessible in terms of compute costs, especially because new classifiers can be trained and used on top of existing high-quality diffusion models.
comments.explanation: The underlying point is reasonable: if a strong diffusion model already exists, training a new classifier for guidance can be cheaper than retraining the generative model itself, and this is an attractive modularity property. I checked this against the earlier method description, where the classifiers are trained on the same noising distribution and are used only at sampling time, so the authors are not claiming that the generator must be retrained. What remains somewhat overstated is the unqualified phrase “more accessible in terms of compute costs.” Guidance adds classifier-gradient computation during every sampling step, and the classifier must be trained for the relevant noisy-image distribution, resolution, and conditioning task. The claim is therefore most clearly true for adapting an existing diffusion model to new guidance signals, not necessarily for end-to-end generation cost or sampling cost. Without that qualification, a reader could incorrectly infer that classifier guidance generally lowers compute requirements.
